title: Good News of Great Joy
author: Dylan Howard
preview: Indeed, the joy of proclaimed to the shepherd in Luke—that is, the pleasure of Christ completing our salvation—is now also our joy. Our sin is gone, and, in its place, is deep and abounding joy.
body: "In the same region, shepherds were staying out in the fields and keeping watch at night over their flock. Then an angel of the Lord stood before them, and the glory of the Lord shone around them, and they were terrified. But the angel said to them, "Don’t be afraid, for look, I proclaim to you good news of great joy that will be for all the people: Today a Savior, who is Messiah the Lord, was born for you in the city of David."
"...I proclaim to you good news of great joy..." – Luke 2:10
Lately, I’ve been so gripped by this section in the story of Jesus’s birth. Take a closer look at what the angel tells the shepherds.

Indeed, this is true, the angel was telling them the greatest of news, which inspires the greatest of joy. Consider the vastness of our sin and the extent of our evil. All too often we sink into the vain pursuits of our flesh. In the midst of this, the Holy One has come to us and not only drank the cup of God’s wrath-that we fully deserved—but He also drank the same bitter cup of sin we loved. The shame we carried, the guilt we carried, but most important of all, the sin we carried, was transferred into one God-man.

Indeed, the joy of proclaimed to the shepherd in Luke—that is, the pleasure of Christ completing our salvation—is now also our joy. Our sin is gone, and, in its place, is deep and abounding joy. This joy is no longer simply a message, but an actual outgrowth of the Spirit living in followers of Christ.
"...Jesus... who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame..." Hebrews 12:2 [Abridged]

Understand, this joy is not necessarily followed by an outburst of emotion, but it is followed by a sense of peace and calm, comfort and warmth, and an intense understanding of God’s beauty, immensity, and continual goodness. This joy—which perseveres all circumstances—is rooted in the promise of God working all things to end in His glory and our joy in Him.

all of your ways end in the delight of all of Your people. You wept and suffered so that we may rejoice, and, now, we are unworthy recipients of Your grace. How great a joy we have! Let us rest in the thought of Your love, pardon for sin, and our future sinless state.

We often belittle Your blood and slight Your love, but can, in repentance, return to You—our endless fountain of grace and joy. Let us dwell on this great news and delight in its comfort and peace. May we always be in awe of this and seek to share it. To Christ be the glory, amen.

title: Life-giving Breath
author: Dylan Howard
preview: God has blessed us with this great gift; we have the fullness of God's life giving power at our finger tips, in our language. We should be full of thanksgiving to God for His Word and we should center our lives on this gift.
body: "15b...[The Scriptures] are able to make you wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus. 16 All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, 17 that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work."

There's so much to talk about here, and I may follow up on this in another letter, but for now, I was to focus on the last half of verse 15 and the fits half of 16. The cool thing here is that God describes all of Scripture as being ‘breathed'. Isn't that a weird way of saying it? If I missed a class, I wouldn't go up to my classmate the next day and ask, "Hey, did the teacher breath out any important information?" If I did, they'd probably move a few seats away from me.

But I see Paul being very deliberate with this word here. This same word is used: in Genesis 1, God breathed life to a lifeless Adam. In Ezekiel 37, God breathes life into a valley full of corpses and brings them to life. This same action of breathing life into dead people is the same action that Paul's describing here. Paul's pointing out that God is breathing life into His Word and that, in effect, His Word is powerful to effect life in those who read/hear it.

How cool is that? That by intaking the Word of God, we are intaking the life that He's breathed into His Word. This is why it's vital for us to take in God's Word—by memorizing it, by reading it, by studying it, by meditating on it, and by praying it—and to cling to it for life in all things. God has blessed us with this great gift; we have the fullness of God's life giving power at our finger tips, in our language. We should be full of thanksgiving to God for His Word and we should center our lives on this gift.
I thank You for Your holy Word, their teachings, promises, directions, light. In them may we learn more of Christ, be enabled to retain His truth and have grace to follow it.

Help us to lift the gates of our souls so that He may come in and show us Himself when we search the Scriptures, for we cannot begin to fathom its depths, and have no ability to comprehend its heights.
By His aid may we be enabled to explore all its truths, love them with all our heart, embrace them with all our power, embed them into our life.
Bless to our souls all grains of truth garnered from Your Word; may they take deep root, be refreshed by heavenly dew, be ripened by heavenly rays, and be harvested to our joy and Your praise.

Help us to gain profit by what we read, as treasure beyond all treasure, a fountain which can replenish our dry hearts, with waters flowing through each of us as an everlasting river sustained by Your Holy Spirit.
Enable us to learn from its pages faithful prayer that grasps the arm of Your almighty power, achieves wonders, obtains blessings, and draws down streams of mercy.

From it show us how our words have often been so unfaithful to You, harmful to our fellow humans, empty of grace, full of folly, and dishonoring to our calling. Then write Your own words upon our hearts and engrave them on our lips, so all glory would be to You in our reading of Your Word!

title: Humble Service
author: Dylan Howard
preview: What I thought was a wise and zealous ambition, after considering these truths, turned out to be me relying on my own intelligence, planning, and power in building God’s kingdom.
body: "We know that all things work together for the good of those who love God: those who are called according to His purpose."

This grieves my spirit. This is such a precious truth—that God works out every single thing for the good of His people. This is so dear to any believer in Christ. The hope of them in the confusion of life. Along with this truth, one more resonates a harmonic note with this: that God controls each and every thing in agreement with His will.
This verse/truth is so used and abused in our Bible-belt culture. So much so that it’s even led to it being the butt of numerous jokes and snarky remarks.
"...according to the purpose of the One who works out everything in agreement with the decision of His will..." – Ephesians 1:11b
Over the past week, I’ve been reminded of this truth, and, by God’s grace, forced to trust in this truth. The Lord has been faithful to remind me of the great treasure this is.

I’ve been burned out on school. I had so many things pile up and in this I felt so overwhelmed with the number of things that I needed to do and the things that I could’ve feasibly do well. On top of this, I read through Johnathan Edward’s resolutions (a list of resolutions he read each day to himself), and came across this:
"Resolved, never to do anything, which I should be afraid to do, if it were the last hour of my life." – Jonathan Edwards

In my mind and my spirit, I began to resent going to school and the classes I had because of their distraction from ministry and because I so easily could be doing other things that I love, like meeting with international students to talk about Jesus or train up some Lambda Chi guys in following Christ. I thought, "Why would I spend my last hour in school? Why would I waste my time like that? If it were my last hour to live, I’d never spend it in school; I’d spend it sharing the gospel among any group of people who’ve never heard."

What I thought was a wise and zealous ambition, after considering these truths, turned out to be me relying on my own intelligence, planning, and power in building God’s kingdom. This is near sighted and self-centered, and prideful. Thinking my decisions can cause more kingdom glory than God’s decisions is plain and simply a darkened and depraved understanding. This is thinking that I know better than God.

We must trust the promise of Romans 8:28. We must trust God with our time and following His will for His purposes. He has promised that each of these things will be for His ultimate glory and our ultimate good. He will build His church for His glory. (Matt. 16:18)
Should I be ok with spending my last hour trusting in God’s purposes? Absolutely.

My inability to understand His methods does not change the promise of the all-knowing, all-powerful, and all-good God of the Universe, it simply means that I’m forced to lean into Him and trust Him in this.
How great is it that God uses such unable people to accomplish His purposes? This is cause for great joy and hope.
You are great and mighty; we are not. You are holy and perfect; we are not. You are infinitely knowledgeable and intelligent; we are not.
We confess that we are in this weak and needy state, but we praise You that you have seen it fit to save us for Your glory.
O Lord, we are so unable to understand your ways. You inspired Paul to write this praise:
both of the wisdom and the knowledge of God!
How unsearchable His judgments and untraceable His ways!"

We join Paul in exclaiming this praise. Despite our inability to understand your methods, let us trust you in these. Let us trust You with our time, our money, our relationships, and every other facet of our lives. Let us commit these to You, knowing that You’re sovereign over all things and that You work out all things for our good.
Let this produce a great peace in our lives, and a supreme trust in You.
Lord, may it be so, not for our sake, but for Christ’s.
To Him be the glory among all people. Amen.

title: Our Cloud of Witnesses
author: Dylan Howard
preview: Luther understood the reality that that we truly are beggars of Christ. He understood that because God demands a righteousness we cannot produce, we are dependent on Him for this righteousness.
body: "Therefore, since we also have such a large cloud of witnesses surrounding us…keeping our eyes on Jesus..."

On October 31, 1517, a man, convinced of the truth of the Scriptures, nailed a list of theses to the church door in Wittenberg, Germany. Change was on the horizon; a mound of hay had been laid down and this man, Martin Luther, had struck the match and lit the fire. Because of this event, October 31, 2017 is marked as the 500th anniversary of the Protestant Reformation.
Now, you may be like, "Great… Dylan's going to write about some boring history lesson...", but hear me out. Actually, hear the words of this verse.
"Therefore, since we have such a large could of witnesses surrounding us..." – Hebrews 12:1a

Paul addressed these Christians in light of the numerous saints that he mentioned in Hebrews 11: Abel, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Joseph, Moses, Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and many, many others. Among these saint who "were approved through their faith" (Hebrews 11:39), now stands our bold brother in Christ, Martin Luther. Although, not only him, but was Luther surrounded by many other reformers—heroes of the faith all over Europe such as Peter Valdo, John Calvin, Zwingli, and such.

Regardless of whether you know each of these brothers and sisters or not, our Lord worked through their lives, and we can rejoice in freely studying and teaching the truths of the Scripture, and not the ‘truths' of the Pope. Among these truths that Luther held dear was the truth that we are in desperate need of Jesus.

The central point of the Reformation and the central point of Luther's convictions was Christ. In Luther's final moments, he was asked by a friend, "Do you want to die standing firm on Christ and the doctrine you have taught?" He answered emphatically, "Yes!" Luther's said: "We are beggars. This is true."

Luther understood the reality that that we truly are beggars of Christ. He understood that because God demands a righteousness we cannot produce, we are dependent on Him for this righteousness. In his words, we need an "alien righteousness". We need a righteousness produced and provided for us by another. Praise God that He has produces and provided this righteousness in Christ! We are now richly-blessed beggars in Christ. In Him, we've been given the Supreme Treasure of the Universe—God, Himself.

So, as we approach Reformation Day, and as we live past it, let us remember the saints who've lived before us, who were obedient slaves to Christ and who joyfully laid their lives down—both by devoting their time and by being martyred for preaching the truth. And, as we remember these brothers and sisters, let their fixation on Christ encourage us in the same way, and to lay our lives down for His sake.
You are unchanging. You see all and know all. You are powerful and Your Word declares that You have orchestrated the movement of all people and nations for Your purposes.

Among these movements is the Reformation and the heroes of our faith that have walked before us. You produced faith in these men that led to devotion to Your eternal cause. Let us walk in remembrance of these men, meditating on their boldness in You, contemplating their joy in affliction, reflecting on how different our lives would be if we followed You the same way.

O Lord, may it be so! We beg You for this obedience, yet we trust that this is produced in time, not all at once. Let us hold our time, our gifts, our talents and skills, even our very lives with loose grips. Let us steward these well for the cause of Christ.

As we walk in humble obedience, let us remember our status before You as beggars. Let us always recognize our need for Your righteousness and let us always praise You for Your gift of this righteousness.
To Christ be the glory among all people. Amen.

title: Future Hope
author: Dylan Howard
preview: I was studying for Sunday school last week, and was thinking through the Lord's Supper. I was reminded of the expectation component of it. That, by taking the Lord's Supper, we look forward to Christ's return.
body: "29 For those He foreknew He also predestined to be conformed to the image of His Son, so that He would be the firstborn among many brothers. 30 And those He predestined, He also called; and those He called, He also justified; and those He justified, He also glorified." Romans 8:29-30
There's many heavy truths in this passage, but I just want to tease out one: the great joy for us in God's glorification of His people.

This passage promises that those God knew before the beginning of time will be conformed to Christ. We could stop there. Paul could have wrapped up his letter. But he didn't. He goes on and lays out the path for them: those who are predestined will be called, those called have been justified, and those justified will be glorified. Notice, these are not maybe's or might's, could's or should's, these are certainties. Those saved by Christ are kept by His Spirit. This should produce a great joy in us.

I was studying for Sunday school last week, and was thinking through the Lord's Supper. I was reminded of the expectation component of it. That, by taking the Lord's Supper, we look forward to Christ's return. I realized how deep a longing this is in my own life; how deeply I long for Christ's return! His return users in numerous things. Among these are: the end of chaotic weather, the end of evil acts of terror, the end of my own selfish desires which conflict with the lordship of Christ.

The last one consumed much of my time. How sweet it is to long for His kingdom's purity (which is assured to His people) when we are so foul in sin. With the hope promised in this passage, we long for His completed work of purification. With this hope, we long to be glorified and doing all things under his direct lordship—for both His glory, and our good. How incredible is it, not that we are simply saved, but kept in salvation.

We sin — Grant that we may never cease grieving because of it, never be content with ourselves, never think we can reach a point of perfection. Kill our envy, command our tongue, trample down our self.

Give us grace to be holy, kind, gentle, pure, peaceable, to live for You and not for self, to copy Your words, acts, spirit, to be transformed into Your likeness, to be consecrated wholly to You, to live entirely to Your glory.

Deliver us from attachment to things unclean, from wrong associations, from the predominance of evil passions, from the sugar of sin as well as its pain, that with self-loathing, deep contrition, earnest heart searching we may come to You, cast ourselves on You, trust in You, cry to You, be delivered by You.

O' God, the Eternal All, help us to know that all things are shadows, but You are substance, all things are quicksand, but You are a mountain, all things are shifting, but You are an anchor, all things are ignorance, but You are wisdom.
Let us continually look forward to Your return as we pursue our earthly mission— full of hope and joy.
To Christ be the glory, along with the Father and the Spirit, among the Church, forever and ever. Amen.

title: The Immeasurable Greatness of God's Power
author: Dylan Howard
preview: Paul is ultimately writing this text we’re going to look at to point the Ephesians to God’s mighty power in their salvation, and not their own.
body: Last summer some friends and I saw some state parks online that we wanted to visit. So, like typical college students without many responsibilities, we got up early one morning and loaded up in a car for a road trip. What we were particularly interested in were these two waterfalls. I could remember visiting different waterfalls around our area when I was younger, but I wanted to visit them and really appreciate the beauty of where we live.
When we got there, the waterfalls were cool, but it was nothing like I had expected. We could walk down the stream, up to the edge, and look over the falls.

A few weeks ago, a friend showed me where she and her family had visited the same waterfalls. However, when they visited, these waterfalls were raging! They couldn’t stand anywhere near where we stood, or they would have been swept away and over the edge. I couldn’t believe it! I was in awe of how swift the water was flowing through there.

What is it about power that puts us in awe? For sure, it shows itself in different ways depending on whether we’re male or female, but we still look at great powerful things and we can’t help but stare in amazement. We can’t help but take it in. In some way, we even feel some level of satisfaction with this. It’s almost as if we’re made to wonder at powerful things.

I think, in a lot of ways, this is the way we ought to interact with God. I think this text that we’re about to study, shows us the massive power of God, and the only proper response is for us to stare in wonder at His power, and surrender ourselves in complete obedience. So, if you have your Bibles, look with me at Ephesians chapter 2.

In the book of Ephesians, Paul is writing this letter to the church members in the Ephesian Church. As Paul finishes this great introduction to his letter where he praises God for all that He’s done, Paul writes down a prayer that he’s praying for these Ephesian Christians. And, essentially, Paul’s praying that these Christians would keep on growing in Christ. Listen to the final thing Paul prays for them because this is the launching pad for the text that we’re looking at right after it:
“I pray that you may know…the immeasurable greatness of His power to us who believe, according to the working of His vast strength.” – Ephesians 1:18a,19 (Modified)

Paul’s praying that these Ephesians Christians would know even more the great power of God in their salvation, and that this would drive them into a greater relationship with God. Paul is ultimately writing this text we’re going to look at to point the Ephesians to God’s mighty power in their salvation, and not their own. To sum up the central theme of the passage, it’s The Immeasurable Greatness of God’s Power in Salvation. Maybe you want it shorter; you can just say God’s Power in Salvation.

“And you were dead in your trespasses and sins in which you previously walked according to the ways of this world, according to the ruler who exercises authority over the lower heavens, the spirit now working in the disobedient. We too all previously lived among them in our fleshly desires, carrying out the inclinations of our flesh and thoughts, and we were by nature children under wrath as the others were also. But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love that He had for us, made us alive with the Messiah even though we were dead in trespasses. You are saved by grace! Together with Christ Jesus He also raised us up and seated us in the heavens, so that in the coming ages He might display the immeasurable riches of His grace through His kindness to us in Christ Jesus. For you are saved by grace through faith, and this is not from yourselves; it is God’s gift— not from works, so that no one can boast. For we are His creation, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared ahead of time so that we should walk in them.” – Ephesians 2:1-10
Now, there’s a lot going on there, but I want to point out just two main truths that this passage shows us: “Our Weakness in Our Salvation”, and “God’s Power in Our Salvation”.
So, before Paul shows the Ephesians the power of God in their salvation, he shows them how great they need God’s salvation.

Think about it this way. Have you all ever been in a really dark room inside and then run outside really quick? Wasn’t it incredibly bright outside? But as you spent some time out there, it started to wear off and you couldn’t really appreciate just how bright it is outside. In the same way, Paul is reminding these people of just how deep they were in darkness before He reminded them of just how great God’s glory is.

Paul fleshes the idea of their weakness out in four key ways: he shows them that they’re dead in their sins, he shows them that they’re in the sins of the world, he shows them that they’re in obedience to Satan, and he shows them that they’re ultimately under God’s wrath.
“And you were dead in your trespasses and sins” – Ephesians 2:1

Normally, when people talk about God’s salvation and our need for it, they talk about how we’ve sinned against God and how we need His forgiveness. This is entirely right, but it’s not the whole picture and it’s not the point of Paul’s statement here to the Ephesians. What he’s saying is that because of their sin, they’ve become dead to God. They’re not just sitting in timeout because they’ve done some wrong; they’re lying down in a morgue because they’ve separated themselves from God.

This is part of the reason we so greatly need God to save us. Think about how weak a dead person is. Can that person cry out to God? Can that person make their way back to Him? Can that person decide to live a better life and then carry it out? Of course not! They’re dead. They’re unable to move, they’re unable to see, they’re unable to feel. They’re entirely senseless to God.
Not only is man dead in their sins, but man is in the world’s sin.
“in which you previously walked according to the ways of this world…” – Ephesians 2:2a
Paul’s talking about how they’re right in step with the world. That the entire world is in this state of death from sin.

And Paul’s not saying that they’ve done some bad things from time to time and that’s resulted in this death. When he says “the ways of this world”, he’s talking about a way of life. This was their lifestyle. This sin and rebellion to God wasn’t just occasional, but they lived in it every moment of their lives.

Now, Paul’s applying this to the world here. He’s pointing out that it’s ordinary to live a life of sin. It makes you fit in, in our world. We’re familiar with drawing some lines between the world’s way and what a Christ follower should do. Lines like, homosexuality, or abortion, or gender identity. And don’t get me wrong, these are great boundaries, but we so often neglect other boundaries and are content to walk hand-in-hand with the world in them. Boundaries like money, or time, or relationships, or education.

Understand, I say this with love and grace, but whether it’s lavishly spending our money on cool toys, wasting our time watching Netflix, only maintaining friendships with people that we enjoy being around, or constantly complaining about school and it’s pointlessness, these are all massively self-centered ways of thinking.

Brother and sisters, that way of thinking is entirely self-centered, entirely of this world and, as we’ll see in a second, entirely of Satan. We are called to think about all of these aspects of our lives in a manner that is centered on God and His glory among all people, and if we fail to do that we fail to follow Christ.
So, not only is man dead in their sins and in the world’s sin, but they’re in obedience to Satan. Paul goes on and says…
“…according to the ruler who exercises authority over the lower heavens, the spirit now working in the disobedient.” – Ephesians 2:2b

What Paul’s showing us is that man is walking in obedience to Satan. All of these things we just mentioned—money, time, relationships, and so much more—in all of them, a man-centered view is right in step with Satan. Obedience in these ways is obedience to Satan, and disobedience to God.
Finally, not only is man dead in their sins, not only is man in the world’s sin, and in obedience to Satan, but man is under the wrath of God. Look at verse 3.

“We too all previously lived among them in our fleshly desires, carrying out the inclinations of our flesh and thoughts, and we were by nature children under wrath as the others were also.” – Ephesians 2:3
Paul is pointing out that because of the vast disobedience of these Ephesian Christians, that they are rightly due God’s wrath.
Paul explains it this way in his letter to the Colossian church:
“…sexual immorality, impurity, lust, evil desire, and greed, which is idolatry. 6 Because of these, God’s wrath comes on the disobedient.” – Colossians 3:5b-6
Understand this, what verse 3 is showing us is that the deepest part of ourselves—the very foundation—is passionate about sin and condemned to God’s wrath.

Now, God’s wrath is so often ignored or looked over to move on to more palatable topics like God’s love or how He blesses the good, but I want to camp out here for a second. Understand this, our chief issue in life isn’t that God is perfectly good and we just aren’t good enough, it’s that God is perfectly good and we are entirely evil.

We like to soften the reality of this or leave this out when we share the Gospel, but the moment we do, we engage in one of the deadliest lies. Understanding God’s wrath on the wicked is absolutely key to our understanding of the Gospel.

So, what we see in these first three verses is that we have aligned ourselves with the sin of the world and with the evil one. We’ve placed our affections and our allegiances in the very things that opposed God. And we’ve so engaged with them that we’ve not just become numb to God, but we’re dead to Him—unable to feel Him: unable to sense His warmth, unable to see His glory, unable to know Him, unable to turn to Him. We’ve created a massive chasm between us and God. And this is the worst part of sin, that we are separated from Him, dead in our sins, due His wrath.
What is our hope? Praise God that that isn’t the end of it. We aren’t just weak and in desperate need of God. He has provided for us, in our need.
So let’s look at this next truth: God’s Power in Our Salvation.

Isn’t that great to hear? Can’t you imagine the Ephesian Christians all gathered around this one guy reading them this letter and as soon as he reads the words “But God,” they erupt in joy and praise.
“But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love that He had for us, made us alive with the Messiah even though we were dead in trespasses. You are saved by grace!” – Ephesians 2:4-5
Brothers and sisters, this is great news! The good news of the Gospel is that God has saved us! He’s freed us from the death and the penalty of sin.
I love the way that Charles Spurgeon put it:
“I have a great need for Christ and I have a great Christ for my need.” – Charles Spurgeon
Now, you might be asking, “How is this even possible? How can God actually save us?”
God has saved us by sending His son Jesus Christ to take our sin and die in our place. 2 Corinthians 5:21 tells us that…
“He made the One who did not know sin to be sin for us, so that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.” – 2 Corinthians 5:21

Even though Christ lived a perfect, sinless life, in obedience to God the Father, Jesus went to the cross, He took every sin of every one of His people, and in exchange He gave us His perfect righteousness. He died the death that we deserved to die, and He rose again to prove that He had defeated all of our sins.
Martin Luther put it this way,

“By a wonderful exchange, our sins are no longer ours but Christ’s, and the righteousness of Christ not Christ’s but ours. He has emptied himself of His righteousness that He might clothe us with it and fill us with it; and He has taken our evils upon himself that He might deliver us from them.” – Martin Luther [Modified]
And now, by His grace, we only need to repent and believe in Him for salvation.

So, I want to point out three quick sub-truths about God’s salvation. Now, we’ve already touched on the first one, but I want to make sure we're clear on it: God has saved us by His grace through faith.
Look at the end of verse 5 again.
Now, jump ahead to verses 8 and 9.
“For you are saved by grace through faith, and this is not from yourselves; it is God’s gift— not from works, so that no one can boast.” – Ephesians 2:8-9

Paul is making it abundantly clear that our salvation is not from ourselves. So, if you missed the entire first truth of this passage—that we are weak and in desperate need of God’s salvation—here it is again. We can’t find any power or goodness in ourselves to save us, and we are desperately dependent on God in our salvation.
He’s done this by His grace. He’s given us what we don’t deserve, that is, salvation from the power of sin and the penalty of sin and, soon to be, the presence of sin.
God applies this salvation to us by faith. Think of it this way, just like a worker uses their tools to do a job, faith is the instrument that God uses to save us.
Now, understand this, faith is a gift from God. Look at the end of verse 8: “it is God’s gift”. Paul makes it explicitly clear to these Ephesians that this is not from us.

We can’t stir up faith in our heart. Faith is not a struggle or strain to get; we cannot muster up faith in ourselves, it must be breathed into us from the Spirit of God. Dr. Martyn Lloyd-Jones said it this way,

“With faith, there is no strain or tension; rather, it has the element of assurance and confidence in it. If there is strain or tension, trying to persuade yourself to keep from doubting, you can be quite sure that it is not faith, faith is not the law of mathematical probability, faith is not natural, faith is spiritual, the gift of God. You cannot command faith at will, faith is always something that is given by God." – Martyn Lloyd-Jones [Modified]
Isn’t that a beautiful truth? Praise God for giving us great gifts like faith.

You know, I was talking with a guy last week, who’s in the fraternity that I help lead a bible study with. He was retelling me about how all last semester he wasn’t a believer and he was wrestling with doubt and faith. He thought he might be saved, but he didn’t really know. But, this summer he was visiting a church with his parents, and he said he couldn’t think of any other way to describe it, but there was a heavy burden on him as the preacher was sharing the Gospel. He said at that moment, it wasn’t a struggle anymore. He didn’t have to strain for faith in God’s salvation, he believed. Praise God that, at that moment, he was given faith as a gift. He was saved, not from himself, but from God!
This is why Paul is clear to point out that salvation through faith it’s something we can’t boast in. Look at verse 9.
“Not from works, so that no one can boast.” – Ephesians 2:9

No one can boast in their salvation because it’s entirely from God. We are entirely dependent on God at each and every step of our salvation. And rightly understood, the Gospel produces deep humility in us and deep thanksgiving to God.
So, not only does God save us by His grace through faith, but God saves us for His glory. Look at verses 6 and 7.

“Together with Christ Jesus He also raised us up and seated us in the heavens, so that in the coming ages He might display the immeasurable riches of His grace through His kindness to us in Christ Jesus.” – Ephesians 2:6-7
This defeats any kind of self-centered Gospel. God used broken and weak, and, in all reality, dead people to show His great power. Just look at verse 7,
“so that in the coming ages He might display the immeasurable riches of His grace” – Ephesians 2:7
This ought to kill any thought in our minds that God saves us because He needs us for something.

We are not at the center of the universe, God is. We are not at the center of our salvation, God is. We are not at the center of God’s plan, God is. At the center and pinnacle of all things good is God, to whom belongs all the glory and praise forever for His wonderful mercy and grace that He’s shown us. And guess what, He WILL receive all the glory and praise.
So, not only does God save us by His grace through the gift of faith, and not only does save us for His glory, but God saves us for good works. Look at verse 10.
“For we are His creation, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared ahead of time so that we should walk in them.” – Ephesians 2:10
Paul’s telling the Ephesian church that their lives are now Christ’s. Paul said it this way in his first letter to the Corinthian church.
“You are not your own, for you were bought at a price. Therefore glorify God in your body.” – 1 Corinthians 6:19b-20
We are His creation; we are His. We were bought at a price for God’s glory. Therefore, our only response to God’s salvation is that we must walk in humble obedience to God.
What are these good works? There are many ways this applies to our lives, but here’s just two big encouragements in light of these truths.
The first encouragement is to rest in this Gospel.
By resting in the Gospel, I mean stop relying on Yourself. As people, we are weak and needy creatures. We can’t save ourselves.

If you’re not a believer, I urge you, believe in this Gospel. We are lost in our sins and we’re separated from God. We’re dead to Him and facing His wrath, but He’s taken the sins of man on Himself on the cross. Believe in Him and He’ll save you.
Turn from your sin. Pray to God. You don’t have to wait for me to get done, you can do it right now in your seat. Ask Him to save you! He’s promised He will!

If you are a believer, we rest in the Gospel in a similar way. We rest in the saving power of Jesus. We rest in the knowledge that we are saved by grace alone through faith alone. We rest in the Gospel by trusting God to use our weaknesses for His glory and that He’ll provide for us in our Spiritual needs through His Holy Spirit.

Just today I was in an evangelistic Bible study with some international students and we were looking at the story of the woman with the bleeding who reached out to Jesus and He healed her. I love the end of that story where Jesus looks to her and says, “Your faith has made you well. Go in peace.” Because of the faith that He gave her, she was healed and she has an immeasurable peace.

Brothers and sisters in Christ, that same peace is ours. Our strength is not found in ourselves, it’s found in Christ and Him alone. He is the Author of our faith, and He is the Finisher of our faith. As Christ followers, we strive for perfect obedience, but if our salvation is dependent on our obedience, we're still doomed. It isn't as if the Christ died for most of our sins, and it's now up to us, with the Holy Spirit to keep the law. Friends, we rest in the knowledge that Christ we are saved by faith alone, and that the Holy Spirit will hold us and will keep sanctifying us.
The second encouragement is to spread this Gospel.

God has provided salvation for us through Jesus Christ, but God, in His sovereignty, has decided to use His Church, to share this good news to others so that they might be saved. Not only that, but God empowered us by His Spirit to do these things. 2 Corinthians 5:20 says,
“Therefore, we are ambassadors for Christ, certain that God is appealing through us. We plead on Christ’s behalf, ‘Be reconciled to God.’” – 2 Corinthians 5:20

As we rejoice in this good news, we have to remember that there are so many people around us who don’t believe in this good news. And even more than that, there are billions of people around the world who haven’t even heard about this good news. I check this week and there’s an estimated 4.3 billion people who have little to no access to the Gospel. Brothers and sisters, we’re obligated to share this Gospel with them. Their eternity rests on it.

Now, you might ask, “Do you really think that they’re going to go to hell if they don’t even get to hear the gospel?” And I would ask you the question, “Do you really think that we can go to heaven if we don’t tell them the Gospel?”

Is it even possible that we can believe that we have the greatest news in the world, their only hope for salvation, and we can be content with not telling them. We can’t hold those two thoughts at the same time. We either believe the Gospel—that we are in great need of Christ and have a great Christ for our need—or we don’t believe the Gospel and we aimlessly hope that these people will be ok, despite being dead in sin and facing God’s wrath.

And as we think about sharing the gospel, we must remember that salvation is from God, and not from us. God has promised to operate through us to save His church. By His Spirit, and by Him alone, are we able to truly share the gospel.

title: The Love of Christ
author: Dylan Howard
preview: As Paul prayed for his beloved brothers and sisters, it is also my deep desire that you’d keep growing in your knowledge of God.
body: "For God is my witness, how deeply I miss all of you with the affection of Christ Jesus. And I pray this: that your love will keep on growing in knowledge and every kind of discernment, so that you can approve the things that are superior and can be pure and blameless in the day of Christ, filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ to the glory and praise of God."

I empathize with Paul’s heart and mind here. Paul loved the Philippian church members deeply and, as he sat imprisoned in Rome and wrote this letter, he remembered them and expressed his affection for them. His affection was not a merely casual acquaintance. In fact, his affection for them surpassed all the affection possible in a world sense, and encompassed his entire being: the deepest depths of both his body and his spirit. Paul’s love for these people was founded in their common bond in Christ (both his love for Christ, and Christ’s love for them), and strengthened by their experiences and encouragement together. This produced an intense longing in Paul for these people.

It was during my drive last night that I came to understand that I have this same affection for you and miss you. As Paul prayed for his beloved brothers and sisters, it is also my deep desire that you’d keep growing in your knowledge of God (not a simple intellectual knowledge, but an understanding of God in your heart, mind, and soul) and that this knowledge would end in greater purity and your increased enjoyment of God. It is my prayer that these letters would help achieve that end: that these would remind you of my commitment, as a brother and friend, to helping you follow Jesus, which is our greatest concern.

Whatever is perfect and pure and good is ultimately from You. I am eternally grateful for them. I’m thankful for Your providence in bringing them into my life, allowing me to know them, and for your provision and care for them.

I come to You in humility, knowing I can’t provide for them. Yet, in my weakness, I stand firmly and powerfully on Your promise that “the prayer of a righteous man is powerful and effective.” In this boldness that is rooted in You, I ask that You would grant them to love You deeply. Allow them to continue growing into You in every way of their life. Let them know You more and let that knowledge of You not result in arrogance or pride, but in love and humility.

Please appoint that this deep love for You would result in them walking in purity and blamelessness, and let them cling to the promise that “blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God.” O Lord, let them see your glory, your beauty, your immensity, and let them be captivated and moved by this. Let them live each second of their lives in perfect view of You.

Merciful Redeemer, when them inevitably fails you, as we all do so often, remind them of Your great grace and finishing work You’ve accomplished in Christ and that this righteousness is sealed to them by Your Holy Spirit. Let them find great joy in this truth. Praise be to You for this great redemption.
All glory be to Christ, along with the Father, and the Holy Spirit, forever. Amen.

title: The Lamb Who is Worthy
author: Dylan Howard
preview: This is cause for great joy and this is ought to be the primary focus of all we do. We don't live easy lives, but we do live joy-filled lives.
body: The scene is opened with the apostle John leading us into God's throne room--the cosmic control center. God sits on his throne surrounded by His colonels and generals: four creatures, twenty-four elders, and a powerful army of angels. The floor around the throne is like crystal and lightning flashes and rumbles.

All eyes are fixed on this scroll that has been sealed seven times. In this scroll are the final chapters of God's fallen creation and His plan for restoration. As the scroll is unrolled, the events take place in real time.
An angel cries loudly, "Who is worthy to open the scroll?" There is silence. Not one creature claims worthiness to open the scroll.
"...and I began to weep loudly because no one was found worthy to open the scroll or to look into it."

John is overwhelmed with the emotion of this moment. No one in sight seems able to resolve this issue. No one is able to open this scroll and with it bring evil into judgement and restoration to the broken creation. As streams of tears flow down the apostle's face, and elder approaches him.
"And one of the elders said to me, 'Weep no more; behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, so that he can open the scroll and its seven seals.'" – Revelation 5:5
The centerpiece of this chapter--the centerpiece of all creation--is Christ. He was slain for poor sinners like us. We are beggars before Him, but we worship Him as the great Giver of life.

Friend, this is great news! This is cause for great joy and this is ought to be the primary focus of all we do. We don't live easy lives, but we do live joy-filled lives. Our joy isn't found in living a life without trial, but our joy is found in delighting in Him--that we have the blessing of seeing and savoring Him who saved us. This is our motivation (fixation on the Cross) towards day-in-day-out, seemingly obscure faithfulness to Him.

We confess that we are slow to learn and weak to do. We stumble over Your truths, and forget them so easily. How often You've reminded us of Your absolute goodness, absolute sovereignty, and absolute knowledge, and how often we've forgotten these things, doubted them, and treaded over them.
O God, have mercy on us. We need Your aid, Your constant provision to Your church. You've promised to provide and care for us, just as one provides and cares for their body.
Thank You for Your these promises. The greatest of which is the hope of the Cross. Let us live with great confidence and great joy in light of this.
Praise be to You, the slain Lamb, the One worthy of opening the scroll.

title: The Supreme Gift-Giver
author: Dylan Howard
preview: If God is the Supreme Gift Giver, God delights in giving good gifts to His people, and all good gifts come from God, then we have great hope in our lives as we face each day.
body: "Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow due to change."

Recently the Lord has profoundly deepened my understanding of His glory as it is revealed around us. Shadows or images are revealed throughout the Bible. The Passover lamb is a shadow of our truer and better Passover Lamb. The Exodus is a shadow of our truer and better deliverance from sin into the promised land of God’s eternal kingdom. Marriage between man and woman is a shadow of our truer and better union with Christ—being brought into joyful companionship with the Trinity. All of these (and more) use presently visible realities—realities that are to be enjoyed by us, thus glorifying God—to cast an image of divine wonders—wonder that magnify these truths to the infinite and perfect degree, thus glorifying God even more. This simultaneous stimulation of both our worldly and spiritual senses greatly deepens our satisfaction in all that God has provided us in our earthly sojourn. It shows both God’s present provision, and His heavenly gifts to come.

However, this is not where the Lord has stopped teaching me. Not only has He deepened my appreciation of these inspired images and shadows, but He’s opened my eyes to the wonder that surrounds me in all areas. Jonathan Edwards explained that the Bible’s use of images and shadows provide us with a framework to understand these images and shadows, and the Lord’s creation provides us with many other images and shadows to interpret with this framework. That is, the Bible provides us with a sort of grammar for images, while creation provides us with the words. While these images, unlike the biblically-provided ones, are uninspired and, therefore, not flawless, they can enhance our seeing and savoring Christ. With both, we unveil the mysteries of heaven in all that surrounds us. This could be as simple as seeing the joy and satisfaction in friendship, but understanding the eternally increasing and infinitely satisfying relationship with God.
I’ve meditated on many of these recently, and the Lord has shown Himself sweeter and sweeter with each passing thought. However, I wanted to write to you about one of these specifically.

More than likely, you’ve heard of the 5 love languages. In case you haven’t, they are: Physical touch, quality time, words of affirmation, acts of service, and gift giving. I’ve had the most trouble with the last of these. I looked down on this means of affection as materialism-driven and woefully-depraved. However, I noticed today that God actually shows His love through gift giving. Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above (James 1:17), from God who gives generously (James 1:15). God lavished His church with many spiritual gifts (1 Corinthians 12:4). God has given faith to His people (Ephesians 2:8). Even the word for "grace"—that is lavished on His elect (Ephesians 1:8)—is derived from the word for "gift". The pinnacle of this all is that God has given His son to redeem His rebellious children. (John 3:16) For me to slight the love language of gift giving is for me to defame the Supreme Gift Giver.

Understanding that God is the Supreme Gift Giver gives us a deep and calming assurance. Consider the hymn lyrics, "come thou fount of every blessing." Or even the doxology, "praise God from whom all blessings flow." These songs—and, I assure you, the praises of heaven also—are laced with language that recognizes and resounds with thankfulness towards God’s gift giving attributes. Furthermore, God doesn’t half-heartedly give good gifts to us. He delights in lavishing us with gifts. (Jeremiah 32:41) His whole heart and soul are involved in His giving. Now, God’s gift giving is primarily for His own delight in Himself, and this is not the letter to explain this matter, but understand that we are joyful and fortunate beneficiaries in God’s master plan to satisfy Himself in His glory.

What does this mean for our lives though? What does it look like to live this out? This is where I so often fail. My heart is slow to learn and weak to put into motion these profound promises. If God is the Supreme Gift Giver, God delights in giving good gifts to His people, and all good gifts come from God, then we have great hope in our lives as we face each day. Even now, when I struggle with anxiousness regarding possibilities in the near future, I can rest in the knowledge that these possibilities, if they are truly good, will be given by God, from whom all blessings flow freely. I am also assured that, if these desires are not truly best, they won’t be given.
In short, recognizing God as the Supreme Gift Giver causes deep rest in His children as they sojourn with a boundless optimism—an optimism that is buttressed by God’s sovereignty good.

Your ways know no change. Your goodness knows no limit. Your love knows no end. You delight in lavishing Your people with good gifts. These gifts aren’t merely the fulfillment of our shallow materialistic cravings, but these gifts are the true and sure fulfillment of our soul’s deepest longings. To know You is our greatest joy, but our hearts are hard to change, our minds are slow to learn, and our flesh is weak to follow.

Lord, according to Your vast mercy and steadfast love, give us a greater insight into this heavenly reality. Let us be overwhelmed with optimism as we rest in the promises You have given to us. Let us not be discouraged by doubt, but strengthen by the knowledge of Your goodness. Let us not be ravaged by unbelief, but empowered by confident hope that You sovereignly operate all things for Your maximum glory and our maximum good.
To You be all glory among all people through all generations.

title: Broken and Clean
author: Dylan Howard
preview: The Advent season officially begins on the fourth Sunday before Christmas and continues until Christmas Eve or Day. There are a variety of ways to celebrate Advent depending on tradition and background.
body: The Advent season officially begins on the fourth Sunday before Christmas and continues until Christmas Eve or Day. There are a variety of ways to celebrate Advent depending on tradition and background. One tradition is to use an Advent calendar
Enjoy this resource and the anticipation of Christ.

title: The Fountain of All Joy
author: Dylan Howard
preview: If we followed all other commands, but failed to delight in God, wouldn’t we have severed our emotions from our actions, our spirit from our body? So, why would we not delight in Him?
body: "Delight yourself in the Lord, and he will give you the desires of your heart."

Think of the commands of the Bible. Repent. Go. Teach. Declare. There are several others, but how often do we consider the imperative, "Delight". Many entirely disregard this command. Go, teach, declare, but don’t dare delight, praise, or rejoice. (c.f. Psalm 16:11, Philippians 4:4) Why would we do this? If the greatest pleasure we can experience is in God, why wouldn’t we seek pleasure in Him? Wouldn’t this leave us unfulfilled? If we followed all other commands, but failed to delight in God, wouldn’t we have severed our emotions from our actions, our spirit from our body? So, why would we not delight in Him?

Likely, because we are not naturally inclined to love God. People of darkness prefer darkness; illumination is unpleasant to them. The command to delight in God only reveals our decayed hearts—cold and dead from sin. In our own power, we are unable to stir up the affections needed to truly delight in God. We reject this imperative because it undermines our own glory, but this does not remove our need for a new heart with God-centered desires.

The beauty of the gospel is that, in Christ, God fulfilled this command to delight and now gives us new hearts to do the same—hearts that love Him, treasure Him, adore Him, praise Him as the only Almighty Eternal God who is worthy of all praise from all people of all generations. (Ezekiel 36:26, Jeremiah 32:38-41, Philippians 2:5-11) Though we failed to keep this command God completed it. Though we are weak, God empowers us to follow it. Our desire are no longer dead, cold desires. They are lively and God-centered. By grace, delight in God.

You, Yourself, delight in Your infinite perfection. In Your joy of Your glory, You have made us this same way. Our greatest satisfaction is found in You alone. Despite this, we have turned from Your glory to promote our own. We have sinned against Your infinite worthiness and separated ourselves from the satisfaction of Your presence.

Praise be to You that, in and for Your joy and glory, You have given us new hearts to love, adore, and treasure You as supreme. Let us grow in our affections for You and know the infinite goodness and pleasures You offer. Let these affections result in love for our neighbors, and vigilant effort for Your Kingdom.

title: He Wields All
author: Dylan Howard
preview: Indeed, we have an almighty God who exercises His authority over political powers and economic forces to sovereignly promote His glorious redemptive work. His ways are truly beyond our comprehension.
body: 1 In those days a decree went out from Caesar Augustus that all the world should be registered. 2 This was the first registration when Quirinius was governor of Syria. 3 And all went to be registered, each to his own town. 4 And Joseph also went up from Galilee, from the town of Nazareth, to Judea, to the city of David, which is called Bethlehem, because he was of the house and lineage of David, 5 to be registered with Mary, his betrothed, who was with child. 6 And while they were there, the time came for her to give birth. 7 And she gave birth to her firstborn son and wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger, because there was no place for them in the inn.

Consider the circumstances in this story. Caesar Augustus declared a census. Mary was almost ready to give birth. Joseph (a descendent of David) betrothed Mary. God threaded all these together at the perfect time. He did this to fulfill His Word in Micah 5:2. Isn’t this peculiar?

This is a rich example of God’s sovereignty. Namely, that God pre-ordained these perfect circumstances. God put it in the heart of Caesar to conduct a census so that He would eventually redeem His people. We see that God wields mighty empires in His hands to glorify Himself. He controls the political powers of the world to bring immense joy to His people. Solomon spoke to this when he said,
The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of the Lord; he turns it wherever he will.

I learned this past week that a few years ago, in a North African kingdom, an intense persecution broke out against the Church. Islam reigned in this country and they sought to exterminate everything else. The persecution was especially brutal for the native believers. They suffered well for the name of Christ. They proclaimed salvation in Christ alone throughout it all, but they suffered. This story is common in the world, and rightly inspires prayer for the persecuted Church. However, if we’re not careful, we can miss the beauty of God’s sovereign work.

Prior to this, most people in this country believed that it was impossible for Muslims to become Christians. Now, because of the mistreatment, slander, and so on, they are very aware that Muslims can, and have, become Christians. They are interested in these stories of conversion and are more open to hearing Gospel truths.
This story rings with the words of Joseph in Genesis 50:20,
As for you, you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring it about that many people should be kept alive, as they are today.

Indeed, we have an almighty God who exercises His authority over political powers and economic forces to sovereignly promote His glorious redemptive work. His ways are truly beyond our comprehension. He often employs suffering and hardship—that is, He sovereignly allows such events—to bring exceeding joy to His people and build His church. What the world—and its father, Satan—intend for destruction and pain, the Lord powerfully uses to give everlasting life and joy.

Isn’t this good news for us? That God is powerful over even the strongest of systems, and that He wields them in His hand to care even the smallest and most insignificant citizens of His kingdom. God orchestrated the entire Roman empire to move two nobodies 70 miles to a small dirty town outside of Jerusalem. Take heart, our King is mighty over all other kings. They will one day bow before Him, and we will have rest as we behold Christ’s glory. But, until then, let us hold to the faith with steadfast hope. Let us rest that all things are under Christ, our Lord.

ALMIGHTY GOD, You alone are worthy of worship. All else are idols. In the fullness of Your love, You have become truly man to bear the shame of many and redeem them on the cross. By Your death, many live. Hallelujah!

FATHER, while this truth is great, so many have not heard, and so many are under persecution for this truth. They live under the oppression of Islam, in the darkness of a system that demands their fearful obedience to gain favor from a false god.

O GOD, would You save them? Would You show them the depth of their sinful darkness and the bright glory of Your holiness? Let them mourn over the great gap between these two. But, God, in the next breath, breathe life into them. Let them rejoice in the knowledge that You have provided a righteousness that is not our own, but Christ’s. Let them treasure Him. Let them grow in love and faith. Let them persevere with the steadfast hope of their reward—the reward of beholding You throughout eternity.
I ask these things for Your glory by the mighty name of Christ, Amen.

title: Light Dwells With Him
author: Dylan Howard
preview: God is supreme over all—light and dark, good and evil—yet, in His kindness, He’s came to dwell with darkness for a time, so that He may become darkness, and defeat it. He’s redeemed His people from darkness to light, from death to life.
body: What the king is asking is so difficult that no one can make it known to him except the gods, whose dwelling place is not with mortals.

The opening chapters of Daniel overflow with the God’s supremacy over all. Daniel and his friends were compelled to not eat the unclean food (1:8), but God granted them favor and compassion (1:9), and unnatural health (1:15). Daniel and his friends were trained for the king’s courts, and the Lord gave them superior knowledge and understanding (1:17,19). Over and over again, God displayed His multifaceted excellence.

Again, in chapter two, God prepared an opportunity to display His supremacy further. The king has a dream. Now, he wants an interpretation, but he wants a true interpretation, so he keeps the dream secret and demands that his interpreters tell him his dream and the interpretation.

Now, this task is certainly outside of man’s reach, but perfectly within God’s. The king’s usual interpreters recognize this, but with error. They say, "how can we do this? Only gods can and they’re far away. There’s no way this can be done" (2:11).

This statement offers a challenge that fills out the display of God’s supremacy so far in Daniel. Yes, God is immeasurable in power and wisdom, but He is not remove from His people. He dwells with them. He is close by and intimately involved in their lives.

After this statement, the king began to execute these men (who conspired to lie to him about the dream). Eventually, Daniel and his friends plead for the Lord to show mercy, and He reveals the dream and its interpretation to them. In response Daniel praises God. Notice this phrase in Daniel’s praise,
He knows what is in the darkness

God is supreme over all—light and dark, good and evil—yet, in His kindness, He’s came to dwell with darkness for a time, so that He may become darkness, and defeat it. He’s redeemed His people from darkness to light, from death to life.

The king’s dream, desire, and precautions. Daniel’s true interpretation and praise. All of these were orchestrated by God to bring glory to His name and good to His people. Take heart, God’s perfect and unwavering sovereignty and desire for His glory is a rock-solid foundation for our hope in salvation. This is the message of Christmas: we have a God who dwelled with us, so that His people may dwell with Him.
May Your name be honored throughout all generations,
You reign supreme over all. Nations and kings rise and fall at Your will.
None are wise, unless You, the Spring of All Wisdom and Knowledge, flows into them. None are powerful, unless You, the Great Builder, fortify and strengthen them.
In Your manifold perfections, You’ve exceeded all earthly understanding of Your glory.
In Your goodness, You’ve judged our sin, but in Your mercy, You’ve judged it on Christ.
In Your grace, You’ve given us life and peace, if only we turn from our sin and trust in You.
May we rest in the peace of Your absolute sovereignty, and tender intimacy. May Your word reign supreme in our lives, as we treasure Christ more fully. I ask this in His name, Amen.

title: A Lunchtime Prayer
author: Dylan Howard
preview: This food doesn’t need flavors, nor does my tongue need taste. Yet You’ve richly blessed me with this gift. Let me enjoy this food. The flavors, the texture, to temperature. Let it remind me of You abundant goodness–the gifts that You’ve lavished me with, especially, the gift of Christ.
body: You called me to rise once again this morning, to go out, to labor, to communicate, to learn, to study, to advance Your kingdom, to grow in holiness.
Now, I sit to enjoy this meal You’ve given me. By Your grace alone, I am able to sit here, to enjoy this food, to have my body restored with nourishment and grown.

This food doesn’t need flavors, nor does my tongue need taste. Yet You’ve richly blessed me with this gift. Let me enjoy this food. The flavors, the texture, to temperature. Let it remind me of You abundant goodness–the gifts that You’ve lavished me with, especially, the gift of Christ.
His blood is the blood of the incarnate God–infinite in value. How great the cost of my sin!
O God, let me walk in humility, covered in Christ’s righteousness, being conformed to His likeness, rejoicing in the goodness and glory of Your gospel.

title: Does God Keep His Promises?
author: Dylan Howard
preview: You see, God didn't only fulfill His promise to Simeon–that Simeon would see the Christ–God also fulfilled His promise of salvation to His people. He did this by sending His Son the die in our place. Christ made peace through the blood of His cross.
body: If you've been with us the past few weeks you'll know that we're in a series looking at the different characters surrounding the birth of Christ. We've looked at Mary, the Shepherds, and Herod, and now we find ourselves looking at Simeon in Luke chapter 2.

As I was reflecting on this past year, in some ways, it felt like it passed quickly. But as I thought more, I realized just how much had happened in this year. Our new president was inaugurated, all of the unrest that came with that change, and even the guilty pleas of illegal activities surrounding his campaign.
The great tension between the United States and North Korea, and the intensifying contest of missile testing and political rhetoric.

The massacres in Las Vegas and Texas leaving so many families and communities devastated, while others began to question their own security from more events like this. Terrorist attacks all across Europe. Hurricanes absolutely obliterating cities and entire islands.

So much in our world is changing and shifting. The non-Christian looks at all of these things and asks: how can anything be stable? Is there anywhere I can rest on? Is there anything I can really rely on?
The Christian looks at all of these events and has to answer a similar question: How are we supposed to rest in God in this? Does God really keep His promises?
So as we look at Simeon in Luke chapter 2, let us keep these questions in mind: Does God keep His promises? And if God does keep His promises, can we rest securely on those promises?
So, I invite you to look with me to Luke chapter 2, verses 25 through 35.

Now there was a man in Jerusalem, whose name was Simeon, and this man was righteous and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel, and the Holy Spirit was upon him. And it had been revealed to him by the Holy Spirit that he would not see death before he had seen the Lord's Christ. And he came in the Spirit into the temple, and when the parents brought in the child Jesus, to do for him according to the custom of the Law, he took him up in his arms and blessed God and said,
So, with the question in mind, "does God keep his promises," I want us to notice two main ideas. The first is that God kept his promise and the second is how God intended to fulfill His promise.
So, to the first point, notice that God kept his promise.

Verse 25 opens with a description of a man named Simeon. There's not a whole lot of information on Him. He appears briefly. He shows up here in verse 25. And after verse 35, Simeon isn't talked about anymore. Inside of these short ten verses, all the biographical data we're given is that he just happened to be in Jerusalem. As far as his character, we know that he was a righteous and devout man. Righteous in his dealings with people, and devout in his dealings with God. However, there's one unique fact about Simeon that helps us with our question of whether or not God keeps His promises. Look at verse 26.
So God told Simeon that, before he dies, he would see the Messiah. But what did Simeon do with that promise? Verse 25 tells us that he waited. He looked forward to it.

One on the primary lessons that the Advent season teaches is the necessity of waiting. We don't know how long Simeon had been waiting. It could have been days, weeks, months, years, even his entire life, but God's people had been waiting on this promise for centuries.

They had been waiting from almost the beginning of time for a Savior that God had promised. Since the fall of Adam and Eve in the garden–the first sin of man–God had promised a Savior that would descend from Eve and redeem them. God's people anticipated and longed for God's promise to be fulfilled. Eve's reactions to both Cain's and Seth's birth in Genesis 4 showed she was looking for the seed to bruise the serpents head that God promised in Genesis 3. She knew the Savior would be a descendent of hers, so she looked at each of them with great anticipation, but the Lord required her to wait.
Then, in Genesis 5, when Noah's father reacted to His birth, he showed that he was looking forward to the same promise. Don't turn there, but listen to Genesis,
Noah's father was looking forward to the fulfillment of God's promise, and he hoped his son would be able to restore the land that was cursed since the sin of man in the garden, but he had to wait.
We can look and see that God promised His comfort through His prophets. Through Isaiah, He said,
During Jesus's ministry, even He made the comment,
God made His promise to His people, but they had to wait. Throughout the Bible, God's people longed to see this promise fulfilled, and slowly but surely, God revealed the fulfillment of His promise.

Simeon's short story in the Scriptures is right in the transition between waiting, and seeing. Up until this point many people had been longing to see the Messiah, but Simeon, by God's grace, was promised that he would eventually see him before death.

Just to make a quick comment about this, isn't it remarkable how long God's people were made to wait? At the same time, isn't it fascinating how short of a time we wait before we become disinterested with a promise? Let me just quickly encourage you. As you'll see, many people in Scripture waited. God's intentions in waiting are for our holiness. Lean into waiting and lean into the pursuit of holiness.
But, like I said, Simeon was promised by God that he would see the Messiah. So let's see if God stayed true to His word. Look at verses 27.

Peculiar, isn't it? It wasn't as if Simeon had heard of this baby Jesus and came to meet Him. It wasn't as if the shepherds' news from the month before had caused Simeon to be interested in this particular baby. But the Scripture says, "Simeon came in the Spirit into the temple".

At the same time, Joseph and Mary were just carrying out their lives. They followed the custom of the law to have Jesus circumcised on the 8th day after his birth, and they were in the process of going to both dedicate their firstborn son (according to the law) and offer sacrifices to purify Mary (according to the law).
And what was the result of this divine appointment? God fulfilled His promise to Simeon, and revealed that He had kept His promise to His people.

…he took him up in his arms and blessed God and said, "Lord, now you are letting your servant depart in peace, according to your word; for my eyes have seen your salvation that you have prepared in the presence of all peoples, a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and for glory to your people Israel." [Luke 2:28-32]
God promised that Simeon would see the Messiah, and God kept His promise. Simeon longed to see the Christ, and Simeon saw Him.

It reminds me of a kid around Christmas time. They know that Christmas means presents. One day, they see their parent put up a Christmas tree and they know that the tree is a sign–a promise–that Christmas is soon. But, there are no presents are under it. The kid goes to bed, wakes up, checks the tree; no presents. Brushes their teeth, checks the tree; no presents. Goes to school, comes home, checks the tree; no presents. They go on for weeks. Eventually, one morning, Christmas morning, they wake up, peak around the corner to check the tree one more time, and as they lay eyes on their gift, they're filled with excitement and joy.

Friends, in a much, much greater sense, this is Simeon. Notice this, many people were more than likely around. In fact, Jesus probably wasn't even the only baby that was there to be dedicated that day. But out of all of these people, Simeon, filled with the Spirit, saw the Christ, the promise of God, and was filled with peace.

Now, pay attention this, Simeon saw the incarnate God, and had peace. He saw the Son of God, and had the peace of God. How can this be? Lots of people saw this baby, but none of them are described as having peace. Simeon, by the power of the Spirit, was able to see Christ with spiritual eyes, and recognize Him as the Messiah.
Matthew Henry meditated on it this way, "the eye is not satisfied with seeing, till it [has] see Christ, and then it is [satisfied]."
the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing. [Ecclesiastes 1:8]
But, here we see Simeon, his eyes filled with the Christ, and finally satisfied with seeing.
"Taste and See the Lord is good. Blessed is the man who takes refuge in Him."
We aren't commanded to just acknowledge with our minds that the Lord is good. We are commanded, look! See! Savor! The Lord is good. He is precious and delightful, full of pleasures forevermore.

Friends, if you don't see Christ this way, I urge you, look! Pray that God would show you. Gaze upon Him, in all of His glory, in all of His majesty and perfection. Treasure Him. Delight in Him. Turn away from whatever pleasures you're holding onto and embrace the eternal pleasures of God, just as Simeon embrace this baby Jesus.

Look at verse 28. Simeon said, "now, I can go in peace" implying that before, he didn't have peace. Let me ask this: why might Simeon not have peace? Remember verse 25. Simeon was a righteous and devout man. Why might a righteous and devout man not have peace?
Paul answers this in Romans chapter 3 when he says,

What then? Are we Jews any better off? No, not at all. For we have already charged that all, both Jews and Greeks, are under sin, as it is written: "None is righteous, no, not one; no one understands; no one seeks for God. [Romans 3:9-11]
So we see, Simeon may have been righteous in man's eyes, but failed to meet God's perfect righteousness. This is why Simeon looked forward to God's promise of a Savior.

You see, God didn't only fulfill His promise to Simeon–that Simeon would see the Christ–God also fulfilled His promise of salvation to His people. He did this by sending His Son the die in our place. Christ made peace through the blood of His cross. You don't have to turn to it, but hear Romans 5:1,
Therefore, since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ. [Romans 5:1]
Friends, the only way we for us to stand before a holy God, is to be holy. But for us to be holy, something has to happen with our sin. If God is just, sin must be punished.

This is the beauty of the gospel. God has come, lived a perfect life that we could not live, took the penalty for our sin on the cross, and rose to prove that all of our sins were paid for, and we may freely come to Him in faith.
If you're not a believer in Christ, you may have grown up in church your entire life, but let me urge you, unless you turn from your sin and trying to justify yourself, you face eternal wrath.
Simeon saw the promise of God fulfilled. He saw the Messiah and treasured Him. One old hymn says it well,
I hear the words of love – I gaze upon the blood – I see the mighty sacrifice – and I have peace with God
Friend, the only means of true peace, is the peace of God through Jesus Christ. God has kept His promise and offered salvation to us.
It doesn't end there. Look with me at verse 32. Understand, this salvation isn't offered to just a certain group of people.
…a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and for glory to your people Israel." [Luke 2:32]
Friends, this salvation is offered to each and every person. Every social class, every nation, every ethnicity, every language. Not one person is left out from this offer of peace. Not one.

That's why it's so heart-breaking that out of the 7 billion people in the world, 2 billion of them have little to no access to the gospel. It's a reality for these people that they will born, live, die, and never hear the gospel. Most of them will never even meet a Christian who can teach them the gospel.

That's why it's so important for us to take international mission so seriously. God has offered salvation to all people and commanded us to teach the gospel to all. That's why we give freely to Lottie Moon to support the International Missions Board. That's why we give to the Southern Baptist Convention to help support Seminaries that help to train missionaries and pastors who will labor to advance God's kingdom. That's why we pray, fervently, that God would raise up workers to go and teach the gospel to the nations. We don't just put the names of missionaries at the bottom of our bulletins for looks. They are there to remind us to constantly intercede on their behalf. Not just for safety, we pray that they would be met with a field prepared for harvest by God, and that God would establish and build healthy local churches to carry on His mission.

But we shouldn't neglect our neighbors either. If the gospel is for all people, they're included. Maybe that's something the Lord's convicted you about. If that's so, let me encourage you, there are brothers and sisters here who are members of Salem and would be glad to encourage you and help you in that discipline.

Maybe you could walk across the street, or down the road, and invite a neighbor to dinner. Build a relationship with them. Learn about them. Ask them about their faith. Begin to teach the gospel through seeds of truth. And, hopefully, as the Lord wills, they will turn and trust in Christ.

So, we've seen that God kept His promise to Simeon. Although, even more importantly, He has kept His promise of salvation, and offered it to all people. But what does it look like for God to keep that promise?
I want us to notice how God intends to keep His promise through Jesus. Look at verse 34 and 35.

34 And Simeon blessed them and said to Mary his mother, "Behold, this child is appointed for the fall and rising of many in Israel, and for a sign that is opposed 35 (and a sword will pierce through your own soul), so that thoughts from many hearts may be revealed."

We see here something of the cost that God has paid to fulfill His promise. We see the great torture that Christ has to endure to save His people. Two main points are mentioned in these verses: the certainty of Christ's sacrifice and the ends (intent, results) of Christ's sacrifice.
To address the first point, we see that God has appointed His Son to be the sacrifice. What does this mean that this child is appointed for something? Turn with me, to Acts chapter 4.

In this, the apostles Peter and John had just been arrested and interrogated for healing a man on the sabbath in the name of Jesus. When they are released, they are threatened not to speak in Christ's name anymore.
So, when they get back to the other followers, they join together and pray, but notice what they pray in verse 27. They're praising God and say,

…for truly in this city there were gathered together against your holy servant Jesus, whom you anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, along with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel, to do whatever your hand and your plan had predestined to take place. [Acts 4:27]
We see here, very clearly, that God had planned the condemnation of His son. He planned to use the evil of Herod and Pontius Pilate, and everyone else, to condemn the blameless Messiah.

Friends, God's sovereignty is not a problem for us to solve, it's a truth that we are given to rest in. As we see here, it's the foundation of the salvation that God has provided. It's essential to Him keeping His promise. It wasn't that some rulers were out of control and killed the living God. God wielded their hatred to offer His Son as a sacrifice. Proverbs tell us,
The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of the Lord; he turns it wherever he will. [Proverbs 21:1]
God, in His sovereignty, planned to use the evil hearts of the rulers so that Christ would be delivered once, for all the saints. (Hebrews 10:10)

This gives us a powerful confidence in facing a changing world. Knowing that we rest in God who exercises absolute control over it, gives us a supreme hope.
